docs.text: Home Introduction Hello, this site serves as an outlet for me to release some pent-up creative (and sometimes useless energy). I like to tinker in numerous things and hopefully someone will find my posts and notes helpful. It's mostly technical but will also be occasionally be hobby related. My posts are broken into two types, blogs and documentation. Documentation will be broken into relevant collections. They should all be in the above navigation links. About Me I’m a seasoned software architect with over 20 years of progressive experience in ERP systems and technology. After graduating from UCLA, I began my career in technology which has led me to the good fortune of working with amazing organizations and professionals, both here and abroad. At my current position as a contractor for one of the largest retail company in the world, we designed and implemented an inventory management event system that processes over 2.5 millions events a day. To reach me, click the lower right to hit one of my social media.

docs.text: Dominoes Introduction My friends and I have been looking for a good online dominoes game to keep us occupy during quarantine, but we couldn't find any. So I'm creating a pet project. It also serves the following purposes. Allows me to design and develop for fun Keeps me updated in interesting software methodology Keeps me updated in interesting technology Keep the cost low Production grade code Allows me to keep a handy toolkit in one place. Dominoes have many variations and the one I play the most is called All Fives . A variation to it is we don't play teams (we wouldn't be able to stop cheating). I fully believe quarantine will be over and I'll had over the code to my grand-kids before I'll ever get it done. Software Architecture One of the main purpose of this project to better define and document my software architecture style. This includes the following. Architecture structure Architecture characteristics Architecture decisions Design principles Technology These are a few technologies I want to implement and use. Programming Language/Framework. Example is .NET Core IDE. Example is Rider Runs Kubenetes. Maybe on a Raspberry Pi Applications management. Example is Kubernetes . Repository. Example is GitHub . CI/CD. An example is GitLab CI/CD Event streaming technology. An example is Kafka ] Datastore. An example is PostgreSQL External Logging System. An example is Splunk APM. An example would be New Relic Code Analysis Software. An example is Sonarqube Conclusion I'll have blog posts on the various software architecture and technology. I'll mix and match the conversation.

docs.text: Functional Requirements Introduction I'm a proponent of Behavior-driven development with lots of collaboration between all the stakeholders. The outcome of this collaboration is our functional requirements for the project. I've had good experience using Gherkin as the ubiquitous language. And as I'm the only stakeholder (so far) on this project, I get to talk to myself a lot in Gherkin. At least it's not Klingon. The functional requirements are a set of features. Each feature has a use case then a set of scenarios. These requirements will translate into our acceptance criteria down the road. Here's an example. Feature: Create a game As a player I want to create a dominoes game with other friends so I can enjoy an activity with friends Scenario 1: Player starts a game Given a player When she starts a new game And she sets some game options Then a new game is created And a unique identifier is returned And she can send the identifier to her friends Scenario 2: Player joins a game Given a player And he receives a game identifier When he uses the game identifier Then he'll join an existing game You can view the complete requirements here . Given these requirements, we can start working on the architecture characteristics of the project.

docs.text: Architecture Characteristics Introduction Architecture Characteristics are the quality attributes that contribute to the success of the system. Many people call them non-functional requirements, but I don't like to call things by the negative of something else. It makes them feel not as important as functional requirements. I've seen many projects where these characteristics are either only discussed implicitly when discussing the functional requirements. Or at the end of the development cycle when it's time to create health dashboards and alerts. These characteristics are critical to the application's success and may heavily influence the architecture. Make sure the design accounts for them. Examples of Architecture Characteristics Here's are a few examples. Performance Scalability Recoverability Accessibility Security You can find many more on Wikipedia . Many of these terms are ambiguous and overlap with one another. Therefore, it is important that all stakeholders have the same general understanding on the meanings. Identify Architecture Characteristics I found the best way to identify architecture characteristics is from the following. Extract them implicitly or explicitly from the functional requirements Discuss with the domain stakeholders about what they deem as important attributes Personal domain knowledge The requirements may provide explicitly ( Website must respond back quickly ) or implicit ( User satisfaction is extremely important ) characteristics. It's also important to talk to the domain stakeholders to find out what they consider to be important attributes that wasn't in the requirements. Lastly, I lean upon my experience as a developer, architect, and the many late night dominoes games in the college dorms, early adulthood, adulthood, and middle-age years to understand what's important. Using Architecture Characteristics The Architecture Characteristics help shape the final system architecture. Therefore, try to keep the final list as short as possible; each characteristics complicates the overall system design. Also, similar to how functional requirements have acceptance tests, with some more detail analysis we can create architecture fitness functions to determine if the system fulfills these Architecture Characteristics. More on this on a later blog. View the project Architecture Characteristics here .

docs.text: Components Introduction With the functional requirements and architecture characteristics in place, the next step in the architecture design is to come up with the high level components and determine how we want to partition the system. Components Discovery There are a few ways to discover components. Actor/Action C4 Event Storming I'm leaning toward Domain Driven Design and an Event Driven System, so Event Storming is a natural technique to explore the problem space in more detail. Roles We have the following roles. Admin Player : The player that creates the game. This role sets the game options and may adjust some options once the game has started. Admin Player has all the permissions of Player. Player : The other players in the game. System : The Dominoes system will generate events so we may as well create role of it. Commands and Events Actor Command Event Aggregate Admin Player Create Game Game Created Game Player Join Game Game Player Added Game Admin Player Start Game Game Started Game Admin Player Update Options Game Options Updated Game Admin Player Start Round Round Started Round Player Lay Tile Tile Laid Player Scored Player Score Updated Round Completed Game Completed Round Round Game Round Game Player Pick Tile Tile Picked Round Completed Game Completed Round Round Game Player Skip Turn Turn Skipped Round Completed Game Completed Round Round Game Player View Game Game Player View Round Round Conclusion I came up with three roles, ten commands, eleven unique events, and two aggregates. Not too bad. I'm also leaning toward a domain partition. Next up is coming up with the design. And that can mean only one thing....diagrams!

docs.text: Structure Introduction The Architecture Structure is the type of architecture style(s) the system implements. An Architecture Style is a general reusable solution that covers a variety of architecture characteristics. Examples include micro-kernel, event-driven, and micro-services. I'm a proponent of simple architecture styles to fits the need of the current project with some room to growth. And given the information so far, I would probably recommend a layered architecture to get the project up and running as quickly as possible and get valuable feedback. But this project is also but implementing some "enterprise" technology, including Kafka. Therefore, it's going to be much more complex than necessarily. Styles The Architecture Structure is a hybrid Service Based Architecture communicating using an Event Driven Architecture with a broker topology. Details Dominoes UI The dominoes UI will be a web application, probably in React or Angular. I don't have much experience in Frontend development so I'm excited to learn more about this space. API Layer The UI calls the API layer for any requests. The API Layer is the entry point to the backend. Its focus is security and transforming REST API calls into commands. The API Layer can also access the database for queries. Initiating Event Channels The command topics. The API layers publishes these initiating commands. Commands are consumed by the Event Processors. Event Processors These are Kafka processors that do most of the work. Event Channels Event Processors do work and publish events into Event Channels. We'll have dozens of event channels. Dominoes DataStore We'll have one datastore that holds both game and round information. Design Pro Service Based Architecture is more pragmatic than other styles The Service Based Architecture allows me to make the Dominoes System event-driven, without the additional complexity of going Microservices . Evolutionary Event Based Architecture allows us to evolved the application in a straight forward, natural way. We can add additional initiating events without them affecting existing events. Design Con Complexity As noted above, the design is more complex than necessary. DataStore as a possible bottleneck I'm not expecting enough load in the system to make the datastore a bottleneck. But I would assume that it'll be one of the first component that would exhibit performance issues. Unknowns Dominoes UI is a big unknown I've created websites before, but nothing game related. And I have no experience in game or web applications. I guess I could of made the game a command line game, but would you play this? ,---------, ,---------, ,---------, | ' '.' | | .' ' ' | | . ''' | |_'___'_'_| |_'___'_'_| |_____'''_| I don't know how long it'll take to create a functional UI. Dominoes UI receiving events The system is async. Events need to be received by the UI. How do we do that? I'm sure it's a solved problem, but I haven't solved it. Event Processors I have two Event Processors, but there are big questions on whether I need to break them into smaller ones. Should two processors handle multiple event channels and produce dozens of events? Event Processors consumes from both Initiating Event Channels and from Event Channels.
docs.title: 2021-03-08 Structure

docs.text: Infrastructure Introduction The primary infrastructure driver is using Kubernetes to deploy, scale, and manage both the Dominoes system and the development and maintenance of it. Some examples of applications or services deployed to Kubernetes are Database CI/CD runners Logs Collection APM I would like to keep the cost as low as possible. Home Cluster I bought an 8 GB Raspberry Pi 4 for the specific purpose of running a K8s cluster back in mid-2020. Instead of the full Kubernetes, I had it run K3s . I enjoyed the process, but there were a few issues with it. ARM64 The Raspberry Pi 4 uses an arm64 chip. And some popular apps, containers, and Helm charts do not support ARM. Some examples are SonarQube GitLab Runner I would love to run my K8s cluster on a few local Raspberry Pis, but I can easily see myself bogged down for weeks on setting it up and getting it to run various applications and services. Cloud All the major cloud providers has some sort of managed Kubernetes cluster. I'm most interested in Digital Ocean . It's cheap and integrates with GitLab's core components, another piece I was interested in working with. Conclusion I'm leaning toward starting with Digital Ocean and determining if it fits my need and budget. You'll see plenty of more blog posts on this topic. Maybe I'll build a retro gaming machine with my Raspberry Pi....

docs.text: Repository and CI/CD Introduction The next step is to figure where to store the source code. And with the two major players having their own CI/CD, lets add that to the mix. Local One option is running my own git server and CI/CD locally. Setting up a Git server on a Synology NAS is simple. Then add something like TeamCity for CI/CD. The downside of hosting my own Git Server is I'll lose the collaboration coding that a hosted site brings as well as some project management tools. GitLab and GitLab CI/CD My last few corporate projects use GitLab and its CI/CD . I've had good experiences with their CI/CD. It's powerful and flexible. GitHub and Github Actions The other player I'm looking at is GitHub and their Github Actions . I have no experience in Github Actions as it's relatively new. But it sounds interesting and should be comparable to GitLab CI/CD Decision I've decided to go with GitHub. Learning more about GitHub Actions should compliment my existing CI/CD skill set and allows me to compare and contrast the two major players.

docs.text: Milestone 1 Introduction Let's recap where we are right now. Architecture Structure is complete We'll use GitHub as our repository, CI/CD, and project management tool System will be hosted on a Digital Ocean managed Kubernetes cluster This should give us enough to map out our first milestone. Milestone At the end of our first milestone, I want to be able to deploy and test our Rest API in a development environment on our Kubernetes cluster. The REST API will only have one GET endpoint that will return "Hello World". To get here though, there's a lot to do. Tasks Create a Dominoes Project Group and Rest API project in GitHub Add this milestone and these tasks into the Github Project Management Create a simple Rest API with one GET endpoint Dockerize the application Push to GitHub Sign up to Digital Ocean and create a managed Kubernetes cluster Setup CI/CD to the Kubernetes cluster Create a public endpoint Secure the public endpoint Verify the endpoint works That's a lot of tasks. Let's get at them!

docs.text: .NET and IDE Introduction It's been two years since I last worked in .NET. So imagine my surprise when I found out that .NET 5.0 has been out for a few months now. Maybe then went to the Java's upgrade every 6 months mode (Answer: They did not). .NET 5.0 My last experience was with .NET Core 3.1. So I needed a crash coarse on new features and enhancements. Reading through the previous document and What's new in C# 9.0 , the two things that caught my eyes were Records and Source Generator. Records There are numerous benefits to immutable objects and making them easier to create and use is a great thing . Source Generator I enjoy using the Builder Pattern to construct complex objects that may have many different representations. But it's really tedious to create Builder classes for each of your classes. In Java, you can use Lombok and its Builder feature to generate the Builder at compile time. With Source Generator, you should be able to auto generate builders too. Rider In addition to trying out the latest version of .NET, I'll be using Rider to develop the application. I've been a Resharper fanboy for a long time, but Visual Studio and it doesn't play very well together anymore. I've had constant performance issues on a pretty beefy laptop. I've removed Resharper and tried some other addons such as Roslynator and Codemaid. They were good but were always missing some quality of life pieces that after a few weeks I would go back to Resharper. Also, as I've been expanding my technical toolkit, I found that I'm not on Windows as much anymore. My work laptop is a Mac and my desktop has a Linux Mint VM that I spend quite a bit of time in. Visual Studio does have a Mac version, but it's not comparable to the Windows version. I've heard good things about Rider so this is a good time to try it out. Here's a good demo on its capabilities.

docs.text: CI/CD with Digital Ocean Introduction It's been a few days since my last post, but I've been keeping busy. I created the GitHub Repository for the Dominoes API, created a skeleton Web Api, and created the Dockerfile. I also created a Kubernetes cluster on DigitalOcean . Now it's time to setup CI/CD to deploy the Web API into the cluster. But before that, let's do a quick recap. Notes on Docker Unlike Visual Studio, Rider doesn't have an option to automatically generate a DockerFile. It's not hard to write by hand and I needed to be familiar with it anyway. It actually took longer to find Microsoft's official Docker images then fill in the DockerFile. I also needed to enable Docker on Rider . Once that happened, I got a nice little UI showing my images and containers. CI/CD I'm using this doc to help set up CI/CD. There was a lot of back and forth on running the script to make sure all the steps works. After a dozen failed builds, this is the final working workflow file . Some notes on the process. Test on Feature Branch When testing GitHub Action, I recommend running it on a feature branch so the main branch commits doesn't get pollute with small failed commits. It took me 11 tries before I got it right. To have this action run on every push to all feature branches change branches: [ main ] to branches: - '**' Image Tag The script will replace the <IMAGE> token in the Kubernetes deployment file with the published Docker image tag. Before this, I used to tag my images with latest , but this is a nice way to solve the problem of figuring out which image to pull. DigitalOcean Kubernetes Cluster Creating a Kubernetes cluster on DigitalOcean is a breeze. It took a few clicks to get up and running. Their Kubernetes console is nice, but you'll still need to know kubetctl to do some work. I also highly recommend Visual Code - Kubernetes Plugin for when you want to click around at stuff. DigitalOcean Cost DigitalOcean can get pricy quick. I received $100 in Promotion Credit that I could use within 60 days for signing up so it's not costing me real money yet, but let's go over the monthly charges. The Kubernetes cluster is 1vCPU with 2GB Memory and 50 GB Disk space costing $10/month. This could probably contain all my applications, but I doubt it could also support a Kafka cluster and PostgreSQL database, APM and logging platform. And I was hoping to add development tools (e.g. Sonarqube) on here. I'm thinking the minimal is 2vCPU and 4GB Memory (of which only 2.5GB is usable), which will run $20/month. $40/month will get you 4vCPU and 8GB Memory (6GB useable) The smallest load balancer costs $10/month. Conclusion It comes out to $30/month or $360/year. Not bad, but more than what I was hoping for. (Stares longingly at my Raspberry Pi 4). Maybe there's another way....

docs.text: CI/CD with Raspberry Pi Introduction My Raspberry Pi has gone through a few stages; first as a game emulator, then a media center, a Kubernetes cluster, a dust bin, and now back to a Kubernetes cluster/Development Tools Box. The setup this time went a lot more smoothly than the first time. Let's go over the steps. Kubernetes Cluster Setup I installed Ubuntu Server ARM64 onto the Raspberry Pi. "Installed" is a strong word as all it required was installing the Raspberry Pi Imager, picking the OS, flashing the SD card, and inserting it into the Pi. I choose Ubuntu Server because I'm expecting my Raspberry Pi to be a server for all sorts of applications and Ubuntu has more support than Raspbian. One additional benefit of using Ubuntu Server is that Canonical created a simple K8s cluster called MicroK8s . It's simple to set up but requires an OS that supports snaps . cgroup The one hiccup is cgroup is required but isn't enabled by default. This page describes the step in enabling cgroup. Addons The default version of MicroK8s is very lightweight. There are plenty of addons to spice it up. I installed dashboard , dns , helm3 , and ingress . Also, fluentd , metrics-server , and prometheus sounds interesting and hopefully I can try them out later. External access The GitHub agent needs to be able to deploy my applications to the Kubernetes cluster, so I need to provide external access. The one gotcha is the Kubernete's certificate doesn't know my external IP address and clients will get warnings about possible invalid certificate when authenticating. To add the external IP address, check out this post . Make sure the IP id is greater than 100. GitHub Actions The updated GitHub Action file shows all the changes to get my CI/CD up and running. Wherever possible, I'm using the GitHub action instead of executing the underlying commands. For example, using Azure/k8s-deploy@v1.4 instead of executing kubectl apply ... . The Actions provides setup and teardown and I wanted to learned how to use actions. All of these actions are open sourced so I can jump into the scripts if I needed to. Container Registry Instead of DigitalOcean's container registry, I'm using DockerHub. The free plan is limited to 2 private repository and 500 megs but that should be enough for this project. GitHub has their on container registry, but it's in beta and I couldn't get it enabled. There's also the option of using MicroK8's built-in registry . Docker Image The Kubernetes cluster is running on ARM64 so the container image must be built for ARM64. I also wanted an AMD64 image built in case I wanted to pull the image and run it locally on my laptop. Fortunately, there's an action for this. In addition, I wanted to the tag the image with the commit SHA instead of using latest . Creating and using variables isn't simple. This does require me to checkout the branch first. Otherwise I wouldn't need to checkout the branch first because build-push-action will do the checkout, build, and push for you. Deploy to Kubernetes There's two actions to deploy to the Kubernetes cluster; setting up the Kubernetes context and then deploying using the manifest file. And after a few days of work, here's the end result. Yes, that's 45 runs before getting this to work. Time to celebrate and grab a diet Coke!
docs.title: 2021-03-27 CI/CD with Raspberry Pi

docs.text: Goodbye Pi Raspberry Pi Issues I wish I could use my Raspberry Pi 4. But there were two major issues I faced in the week I using it as my primary Kubernetes cluster. ARM64 support A lot of applications don't support the ARM64 architecture. For example, there aren't helm charts for Kafka. And Confluent Platform doesn't support ARM64 at all. Support should increase now that the new MacBooks are using ARM64 chips, but it doesn't help me now. Performance The performance is slooooow. Calling a simple Rest API on the Kubernetes cluster takes a minute to warm up and a dozen seconds afterward. And that's only with one pod running in the cluster and Kafka installed on the OS itself. I still had a database and a few other applications I wanted to add onto it. Hello Old Computer Fortunately, I had an old gaming computer laying around. I installed Ubuntu Desktop on it (Server didn't boot up for me) and I'm a pro at installing MicroK8s now. I'm hoping it can handle the workload, but I can always add in additional computers to the cluster. And save on my heating bill! Kafka on Kubernetes It was also easy to install Kafka using Helm chart . I wanted something simple to get up and running. I'll probably move to the Confluent Platform at some point to get Schema Registry. By default, the Kafka cluster is only reachable within the Kubernetes cluster. There's a few ways to provide external access , but I found this to be the easiest way if you only have one Kafka broker. helm upgrade --set externalAccess.enabled=true --set externalAccess.service.type=NodePort --set externalAccess.service.nodePorts[0]='{port}' --set externalAccess.service.domain={internal-ip-address} my-kafka bitnami/kafka I needed to add externalAccess.service.domain and use the internal ip address of the Kubernetes cluster. Otherwise the broker will try to access the external listener by the public IP address and I didn't open it up. To get a Kafka client pod up and running, execute the following commands. kubectl run my-kafka-client --restart='Never' --image docker.io/bitnami/kafka:2.7.0-debian-10-r109 --namespace {namespace} --command -- sleep infinity kubectl exec --tty -i my-kafka-client --namespace {namespace} -- bash Back to the Raspberry Pi All is not lost for the Raspberry Pi though. I can still use it as a database server or for some other dominoes related task. Please don't be a paper weight :(

docs.text: Architecture Characteristics Customizability We'll start off with one type of dominoes game, but we'll provide lots of options to customize it. Some options include Number of players Score to win the game Number of tiles Initial number of tiles in each player's hand Options can run into the mid double digits. And that's only for a single game. We would like to allow for additional types of games, which will require a different set of options. And we've only discussed the game play. There will be another set of options for the UI. Testability Dominoes have many rules. The only way to do make sure the game works as intended without spending tons of resources or make everyone a beta tester is to make sure our game engine has a full suite of automated tests.

docs.text: Functional Requirements Feature: Create a game As a player I want to create a dominoes game with other friends so I can enjoy an activity with friends Scenario 1: Player starts a game Given a player When she starts a new game And she sets some game options Then a new game is created And a unique identifier is returned And she can send the identifier to her friends Scenario 2: Player joins a game Given a player And he receives a game identifier When he uses the game identifier Then he'll join an existing game Scenario 3: All players join the game When all players join the game Then the game starts And a round begins Feature: Play a round As a player I want to play a dominoes round so that I can beat other players Scenario 1: Start a round Given the game has started But a round has not started When a player starts a round Then a new round is created And tiles are given to each player Scenario 2: Player plays her hand Given the round has started And it is Player's X move When she makes a move Then the move is reflected in the round And all players can see the move And if she scores points then the points are reflected Scenario 3: Round completes Given that the player has put down his last dominoes And the round is completed And the game isn't over yet When the round is complete Then another round can begin Feature: Complete a game As a player I want to finish a game so that I can see who won Scenario 1: Game completes Given that the round completes When a player wins the game Then the player is declare the winner And the game is over Feature: Game reset As a player I want to store and come back to an existing game so that I can pick up where I left off Scenario 1: Game continues Given that a game has started And the game hasn't been removed When a player access the game Then the game removal timer is reset Scenario 2: Game removal Given that a game has started And the game hasn't been removed When players have not access the game for a period of time Then the game is removed Feature: Playability As a player I want to play on my computer And on my mobile device So I can play wherever I happen to be Scenario 1: Play on a computer Scenario 2: Play on a mobile device

docs.text: Kubernetes HomeLab Tutorial - Part 1 - Installing Kubernetes and Addons Introduction This is a set of tutorials on setting up a Kubernetes cluster at home. At the end, we'll get the following. Kubernetes cluster CI/CD with Github Actions Kafka Logging using Elastic Search, FluentD, and Kibana Metrics and Alerts using Prometheus and Grafana I'm working on a Dominoes game as a learning experience. The game consists of multiple applications running on Kubernetes and communicating through Kafka. I wanted to automate the CI/CD process and add common tools such as logging and metrics with minimal configuration to each component. I'll keep this tutorial updated with my latest findings. Assumptions For people following along with this tutorial, there's some assumption I've made. You know the basic of Kubernetes and you're comfortable with kubectl You have a spare computer and you're not running an ARM device (sorry Raspberry Pi). Some of the components we'll add will not work on ARM devices. Not For Production The cluster is enough for development, but I wouldn't run my production on it for the following reasons. Single Node The Kubernetes is running on a single system. Though it's easy to add additional nodes with MicroK8s, I'm using a very simple way to allocate storage, and you may need something more complex for a cluster. External traffic using Node Ports Node Port is the simplest way to get external traffic flowing into the Kubernetes cluster. But there's plenty of downsides to it. This article gives a good overview between Node Port, Load Balancer, and Ingress. Rather that deal with the extra complexity of Ingress, I'll use Node Ports. No Authorization The cluster isn't using RBAC Authorization . It's hosted at home with the only open port used for kubectl. Adding RBAC adds additional complexity. Step 1: Installing the OS - Ubuntu The first step is installing the OS. I choose Ubuntu because it makes setting up Microk8s a snap. The Desktop or Server edition are very similar so choose whichever you're comfortable with. Change Host Name I like to give the computer a descriptive hostname (e.g. k8s-staging-server ). Run the following command to update the hostname. hostnamectl set-hostname [new-host-name] Run hostnamectl to verify it worked. SSH Setup I usually SSH into the Kubernetes server from my development laptop. Therefore, I needed to add openssh-server . A nifty option is the ability to import your keys from either launchpad or github. Step 2: Installing Kubernetes - Microk8s Microk8s is a small, fully-conformant Kubernetes that's easy to setup. Installing only requires running the following command. sudo snap install microk8s --classic It'll take a few minutes to install the application. Run microk8s status to confirm everything is up and running. Step 3: Setting up your kubectl Run the following command on the Ubuntu system to get your configuration. microk8s config Copy that information into your development system ~/.kube/config file in whatever method you're most comfortable with. Then try to connect to the Kubernetes cluster from your development system. Certificate error Most likely you'll get a certificate is invalid error. This post describes some possible solutions. The following steps work for me. Edit the certificate template file nano /var/snap/microk8s/current/certs/csr.conf.template and under alt_names add your ips. [ alt_names ] DNS.1 = kubernetes DNS.2 = kubernetes.default DNS.3 = kubernetes.default.svc DNS.4 = kubernetes.default.svc.cluster DNS.5 = kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local IP.1 = 127.0.0.1 IP.2 = 10.151.233.1 IP.100 = <K8S_INTERNAL_IP> IP.101 = <K8S_EXTERNAL_IP> I needed to add both the internal IP (for local access) and external IP (for GitHub CI/CD). The IP IDs must be over 99. Once you save the file, the template is automatically reloaded and you should now be able to connect to the cluster. Kubernetes UI I highly recommend Visual Studio - Kubernetes addon to view your Kubernetes cluster. But don't worry, you'll still need to keep kubectl nearby. Step 4: Addons Next, let's add some addons . dns dashboard helm3 storage The documentation describes the purpose of these addons and how to add them. Kubernetes Dashboard By default, the Kubernetes Dashboard is not accessible from outside the cluster. The simplest way to change that is to create a NodePort for the service Running kubectl get service --namespace kube-system shows the following information. NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE kubernetes-dashboard ClusterIP 10.152.183.197 <none> 443/TCP 82s ... Run the following command to update the service. kubectl edit svc/kubernetes-dashboard --namespace kube-system Then update the port from ClusterIP to NodePort and add the port you want to use. Here's an example. ... ports: - port: 443 protocol: TCP targetPort: 8443 nodePort: 30443 # Pick a port number selector: k8s-app: kubernetes-dashboard sessionAffinity: None type: NodePort # Changed from ClusterIP ... Save the file. The service should be updated. Running kubectl get service --namespace kube-system again should show the following. NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE kubernetes-dashboard NodePort 10.152.183.197 <none> 443:30443/TCP 17m ... Let's access the dashboard by going to your web browser and entering https://[k8s-ip-address]:30443/ . You'll see a Warning page because the SSL certificate is self-signed. Continue on and you'll reach the dashboard. There's two ways to authenticate. If you updated your kubeconfig file then choose that authentication option. Next Steps We started from scratch and now we have a working Kubernetes cluster. For our next step, let's deploy a custom application into the cluster.

Run the following command to update the service. kubectl edit svc/kubernetes-dashboard --namespace kube-system Then update the port from ClusterIP to NodePort and add the port you want to use. Here's an example. ... ports: - port: 443 protocol: TCP targetPort: 8443 nodePort: 30443 # Pick a port number selector: k8s-app: kubernetes-dashboard sessionAffinity: None type: NodePort # Changed from ClusterIP ... Save the file. The service should be updated. Running kubectl get service --namespace kube-system again should show the following. NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE kubernetes-dashboard NodePort 10.152.183.197 <none> 443:30443/TCP 17m ... Let's access the dashboard by going to your web browser and entering https://[k8s-ip-address]:30443/ . You'll see a Warning page because the SSL certificate is self-signed. Continue on and you'll reach the dashboard. There's two ways to authenticate. If you updated your kubeconfig file then choose that authentication option.

docs.text: Kubernetes HomeLab Tutorial - Part 2 - CI/CD with GitHub Actions Introduction In the previous tutorial, we created a Kubernetes cluster using MicroK8s. This tutorial will set up CI/CD using GitHub Actions and have us deploy to the Kubernetes cluster. You can skip this tutorial and go the next one if you're not interested. But I recommend have some custom application running on the Kubernetes cluster for the next tutorials. Assumptions I'm going to make the following assumptions. You have a repository that you want to deploy to your Kubernetes cluster. The repository I created is for an ASP.NET Core application, but most of these steps are applicable for other applications. You know how to dockerize your application. The repository is on GitHub. You know how use GitHub Actions to build and deploy your application Step 1: Open the kubectl port The first step is to update your router settings to allow GitHub to access your Kubernetes cluster using kubectl . By default the port is 16443 . Step 2: Add your KubeConfig as Repository Secret Run the following command to get your kube configuration. microk8s config Go to your repository in GitHub, then to Settings : Secrets . Add a secret named KUBE_CONFIG and paste your configuration. Change the internal IP address to your external IP Address under cluster:server . Save your secret. Step 3: Update your workflow Feel free to look at my repo to see my workflow YAML file . There's enough information to parase through to be its own tutorial. But the relevant steps are highlighted below. ... - name: Set K8s context id: setcontext uses: azure/k8s-set-context@v1 with: method: kubeconfig kubeconfig: ${{ secrets.KUBE_CONFIG }} ... - name: Deploy to Kubernetes cluster uses: Azure/k8s-deploy@v1.4 with: namespace: 'dominoes' manifests: ./WebApplication/config/deployment_updated.yml # Uses the output from Variable Substitution in K8s Deployment file images: 'hqv1/dominoes-api:${{ steps.vars.outputs.sha_short }}' kubectl-version: 'latest The Set K8s context step is where we set the kubeconfig secret to tell the GitHub agent where the Kubernetes cluster is at. The Deploy to Kubernetes cluster does the deployment. You'll notice that we'll deploying to the dominoes namespace. We should create it. Step 4: Create an application namespace It's as simple as kubectl create namespace dominoes Step 5: Run the workflow and verify the result Last step is running the workflow and verifying that the deployment works. Once the workflow completes successfully, let's run the following command. kubectl get pods -n dominoes And the pod should be up and running. NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE dominoes-api-5598cbcf64-v6l2s 1/1 Running 0 92s Conclusion I may have breezed through the CI/CD, but it took me a good week to get everything working as expected. But fortunately, there's enough documentation on GitHub actions that you should be able to get by. And you can always drop me a question if you get stuck. Now on to Kafka.

docs.text: Kubernetes HomeLab Tutorial - Part 3 - Setting up Kafka Introduction In the previous tutorial, we used GitHub Actions to create our CI/CD to deploy to our Kubernetes cluster. Let's get back to the cluster and add some additional components. In this tutorial, we'll be adding Kafka. Kafka Helm Chart Apache Kafka is an event streaming platform. I wanted to use helm charts to install Kafka because it manages the complexity of installing all the various components to make Kafka work. There are two helm chart options. One is the bare-bones Kafka helm chart and the other is the Confluent Platform . Confluent adds additional components such as Kafka Connect, the Schema Registry, and ksqlDB. I was looking for something simple to begin with, so I went with the bare-bones Kafka. The installation page shows the two commands needed to install Kafka. But by default, it doesn't allow external access to the Kafka cluster. In other word, the only way for you to connect to Kafka is inside the Kubernetes cluster. I also wanted to use the Kafka broker for local development on my development system so I needed to figure out a solution. There's a few ways to access the Kafka brokers from outside the cluster . The easiest way is to create a NodePort ; but remember that this will only work with one Kafka broker. The following command will install Kafka and allow external access to the Kafka broker. helm install --namespace dominoes --set externalAccess.enabled=true --set externalAccess.service.type=NodePort --set externalAccess.service.nodePorts[0]='30092' --set externalAccess.service.domain=[k8s-internal-ip-address] my-kafka bitnami/kafka Change the namespace, port number, internal ip address, and deployment name to something that suits you. The externalAccess.service.domain is needed because by default, Kafka will try to route to the broker using the external IP address of the cluster. But because we haven't allowed any outside access, it'll time out. The installation will create various resources. Run kubectl get services to see some. NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE ... my-kafka-zookeeper-headless ClusterIP None <none> 2181/TCP,2888/TCP,3888/TCP 67s my-kafka-headless ClusterIP None <none> 9092/TCP,9093/TCP 67s my-kafka-0-external NodePort 10.152.183.16 <none> 9094:30092/TCP 66s my-kafka-zookeeper ClusterIP 10.152.183.6 <none> 2181/TCP,2888/TCP,3888/TCP 66s my-kafka ClusterIP 10.152.183.235 <none> 9092/TCP 66s Make note of my-kafka-0-external , which is how you'll access the Kafka broker from outside the Kubernetes cluster. Kafka Client Once the pods are up and running, you'll be able to access the Kafka broker. One way to run Kafka commands is to create a pod and use it as a Kafka client. kubectl run my-kafka-client --restart='Never' --image docker.io/bitnami/kafka:2.7.0-debian-10-r109 --namespace dominoes --command -- sleep infinity kubectl exec --tty -i my-kafka-client --namespace dominoes -- bash Once you're in the pod you can create and list topics. kafka-topics.sh --create --topic dominoes-dev --bootstrap-server my-kafka-0.my-kafka-headless.dominoes.svc.cluster.local:9092 kafka-topics.sh --list --bootstrap-server my-kafka-0.my-kafka-headless.dominoes.svc.cluster.local:9092 Accessing the Kafka Broker There's two ways to access the Kafka broker. If you're within the Kafka cluster (as in the previous example) use --bootstrap-server my-kafka-0.my-kafka-headless.dominoes.svc.cluster.local:9092 . If you're on the local network use [internal-ip-address]:30092 . I didn't set up any security on the Kafka Broker so I highly recommend you don't allow public access till you lock it down. Conclusion And with that, you have a Kafka broker in your Kubernetes cluster. Now lets jump into logging.

docs.text: Kubernetes HomeLab Tutorial - Part 4 - Logging with EFK Introduction Let's continue on our journey and install a logging framework. This allows us to consolidated all of our logs into one central location. We'll be using EFK. EFK stands for ElasticSearch-Fluentd-Kibana . It's a logging and monitoring solution. Installation To install EFK, enable the addon by running the following command on the Ubuntu system. microk8s enable fluentd A bunch of resources are created within the kube-system namespace. Run the command kubectl get services --namespace kube-system NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE ... elasticsearch-logging ClusterIP 10.152.183.196 <none> 9200/TCP 113s kibana-logging ClusterIP 10.152.183.106 <none> 5601/TCP 113s Port Forwarding To access Kibana, we'll use kubectl built in port-forwarding command. kubectl -n kube-system port-forward svc/kibana-logging 8001:5601 In this example, I'm using port 8001 . Then on your web browser, go to http://localhost:8001/ and you should see the Kibana welcome page. Creating an index Go to page http://localhost:8001/app/kibana#/management/kibana/index_pattern?_g=() and create an index pattern. I recommend logstash* so you'll pick up all the indexes. Discover And that's it. If your application is writing to the console, Fluentd should be picking it up, storing it in ElasticSearch, and you should be able to query for it in Kibana. Simple and nifty.

docs.text: Kubernetes HomeLab Tutorial - Part 5 - Monitoring and Alerting with Prometheus and Grafana Introduction For the last leg of the HomeLab tutorial, let's add some monitoring and alerting. We'll use Prometheus to monitor our Kubernetes cluster and applications and Grafana to create our dashboards. Installation To install Prometheus and Grafana, run the following command on the Ubuntu system microk8s enable prometheus The installation will create a bunch of resources under the monitoring namespace. Running kubectl get services --namespace monitoring will show the following. NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE prometheus-operator ClusterIP None <none> 8443/TCP 64s alertmanager-main ClusterIP 10.152.183.198 <none> 9093/TCP 60s grafana ClusterIP 10.152.183.76 <none> 3000/TCP 60s kube-state-metrics ClusterIP None <none> 8443/TCP,9443/TCP 60s node-exporter ClusterIP None <none> 9100/TCP 60s prometheus-adapter ClusterIP 10.152.183.52 <none> 443/TCP 60s prometheus-k8s ClusterIP 10.152.183.34 <none> 9090/TCP 59s alertmanager-operated ClusterIP None <none> 9093/TCP,9094/TCP,9094/UDP 37s prometheus-operated ClusterIP None <none> 9090/TCP 36s Setting up our Application to Publish Metrics We'll need to set up our application to create metrics for Prometheus to consume. You can look at this repo to see how it's done. Install the Nuget Package Instructions are on this page . For ASP.NET Core, add the Nuget package prometheus-net.AspNetCore . Configuration Next up is adding HTTP metrics and publishing them to the /metrics endpoint. You'll do both in the Configure method. public void Configure ( IApplicationBuilder app , IWebHostEnvironment env ) { ... app . UseRouting (); app . UseAuthorization (); app . UseHttpMetrics (); // Prometheus app . UseEndpoints ( endpoints => { endpoints . MapControllers (); endpoints . MapMetrics (); // Prometheus }); } Viewing the metrics Now run your application locally and access the \metrics endpoint. Various metrics created by the Prometheus library appears on this page. Deploy the application to Kubernetes With these changes in place, let's deploy the application to Kubernetes. Then we'll set up Prometheus to monitor our application. Port Forwarding Similar to our last tutorial, both Prometheus and Grafana have UI components and require port forwarding to access them. The following command will set up them up. kubectl port-forward -n monitoring service/prometheus-k8s 9000:9090 kubectl port-forward -n monitoring service/grafana 3000:3000 Prometheus: Service Discovery Access the Prometheus UI by going to your web browser and entering http://localhost:9000 . Go to Graph and search for some metrics that our application should be sending. You can go back and look at the local \metrics endpoint for reference. You'll find that none appears. There's some pod related information, but nothing that the application itself is generating. The next important pages I want you to visit is Status->Service Discovery and Status->Target . You'll notice that our application dominoes-api is not on these pages. We'll need to create a scrape config to monitor our application. The important thing to know is that we can't update the Prometheus config directly. When we enabled the Prometheus addon, it created a Prometheus Operator . Each monitor on the Service Discovery page corresponds to a Kubernetes ServiceMonitor . View them by running the following command. kubectl get servicemonitor --namespace monitoring And we'll get the following result. NAME AGE alertmanager 36m grafana 36m kube-state-metrics 36m node-exporter 36m prometheus-adapter 36m prometheus-operator 36m prometheus 36m kube-apiserver 36m coredns 36m kube-controller-manager 36m kube-scheduler 36m kubelet 36m Create a Service Monitor So to monitor our application, we'll need to create a new ServiceMonitor. An example of the YAML file. apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1 kind: ServiceMonitor metadata: name: dominoes-servicemonitor namespace: monitoring labels: app: my-dominoes-servicemonitor spec: selector: matchLabels: product: dominoes namespaceSelector: matchNames: - dominoes endpoints: - interval: 30s This Service Monitor will monitor all services in the dominoes namespace and for services that has a label of product: dominoes . Going back to the application Deployment YAML file, you'll notice that it contains the above label. --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: dominoes-api labels: app: dominoes-api product: dominoes spec: type: NodePort ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 80 nodePort: 30001 selector: app: dominoes-api Save the ServiceMonitor setting into a file and run the following command to create the Service Monitor. kubectl create --namespace monitoring -f .\dominoes-servicemonitor.yaml Review the metrics Let's go back to the Service Discovery page. You should see the monitoring/dominoes-servicemonitor/0 on the page and it should have at least one active target. The target tab will display your endpoint. And the application metrics will show up in the Graph page. Grafana Now let's go to Grafana. Let's create a port forward and then access the UI at http://localhost:3000/ . The default username and password is admin:admin . Go to Configuration->Data Sources and you'll notice that it's already linked to the Prometheus service. Isn't that nice? That means you can start creating dashboards and alerts immediately. Tutorial Conclusion This concludes the creation of a Kubernetes cluster and getting it ready for some serious development work. I hope you enjoyed these tutorials and happy development.

docs.text: IntelliJ IDEA / Gradle and JDK versions Introduction I joined a new team in my workplace last week and they're running a Java/SpringBoot/Gradle setup. I have no experience in Gradle (and my Java skills aren't the best), so I created a new Gradle project in IntelliJ to play around with. This was the setup. Windows 10 Laptop IntellJ Gradle (IntellJ installed version 6.1.1 in ~\.gradle\wrapper\dists ) openjdk-14 The wizard went smoothly but the build failed with a vague error message Could not initialize class org.codehaus.groovy.vmplugin.v7.Java7 . The wizard didn't generate the expected directories and files and was missing needed files like gradle-wrapper.properties . After much searching and random testing I found the issue. Gradle 6.1.1 requires a lower JDK version than 14. I installed JDK 11 and update the Gradle setting to use it. Delete the old project, create a new one, and it worked! Additional Resources IntelliJ Getting Started with Gradle
docs.title: 2020-06-29 Gradle JDK version mismatch
IntelliJ IDEA / Gradle and JDK versions

docs.text: I joined a new team in my workplace last week and they're running a Java/SpringBoot/Gradle setup. I have no experience in Gradle (and my Java skills aren't the best), so I created a new Gradle project in IntelliJ to play around with. This was the setup. Windows 10 Laptop IntellJ Gradle (IntellJ installed version 6.1.1 in ~\.gradle\wrapper\dists ) openjdk-14 The wizard went smoothly but the build failed with a vague error message Could not initialize class org.codehaus.groovy.vmplugin.v7.Java7 . The wizard didn't generate the expected directories and files and was missing needed files like gradle-wrapper.properties . After much searching and random testing I found the issue. Gradle 6.1.1 requires a lower JDK version than 14. I installed JDK 11 and update the Gradle setting to use it. Delete the old project, create a new one, and it worked!

docs.text: Containerized Web API and accessing Azure Key Vault Context Azure Web App Service allows you to deploy a containerized Web API. This is useful because there's many more ways to host a container app than a .NET Web API; and at some point I may want to try them out. Creating a docker container is easy; just the follow the links below. Links Dockerize an existing ASP.NET Core application Microsoft Dotnet Docker tutorial repository Gotchas There was one major gotcha that took me a while to resolve. Azure Managed Identity doesn't work inside a local Docker container This means we can't authenticate to Azure Key Vault using Azure Managed Identity. Here's more discussion and workarounds. The way to get around it is to provide the app with your Azure Token. You'll need to the following. Install Azure CLI and jq Log into azure : az login Get your key vault access token: az account get-access-token --resource=https://vault.azure.net | jq -r .accessToken Add the token somewhere so your app can use it. I added the token in appsettings.Development.json because it's only needed for development. In production, the container will run in Azure Web App Service and it'll be able to authenticate through MSI. Implement reading the token and using it to create the KeyVaultClient . Here's a sample code. var accessToken = builtConfig [ "AZURE_KEY_VAULT_ACCESS_TOKEN" ]; if (! string . IsNullOrEmpty ( accessToken )) { logger . Debug ( "Using Access Token from Environment variable to access Key Vault" ); keyVaultClient = new KeyVaultClient (( string _ , string __ , string ___ ) => Task . FromResult ( accessToken )); } Now that the file appsettings.Development.json contains sensitive information, I removed the file from being tracked in git.
